<tool_call>

</parameter>

This is the value for the second parameter

</parameter>

<IMPORTANT>
Reminder:
- Function calls MUST follow the specified format: an inner <function=...></function> block must be nested within <tool_call></tool_call> XML tags
- Required parameters MUST be specified
- You may provide optional reasoning for your function call in natural language BEFORE the function call, but NOT after
- If there is no function call available, answer the question like normal with your current knowledge and do not tell the user about function calls
</IMPORTANT>

You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo.

## Ground rules when writing code:

- Follow the user's instructions carefully.
- Do not explain answers, just give me code.
- Respect indentation when modifying code.
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Tool use

Never modify files outside of the current working directory unless requested.

Current directory: ~/repos/github/g0t4/dotfiles

## Use `semantic_grep` to find code

The semantic_grep tool:

- has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase
- enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval)
- includes re-ranker
- it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it!
- while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg`

## Committing

- `git diff` is a sanity check!
- COMMIT when you're done.
- Use `--author="qwen3.6-35b-a3b<wes.mcclure+qwen3.6-35b-a3b@gmail.com>"`


## 🙌  TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS

- 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents
- 🙌 `fd` to search file names

## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS

If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom.

- ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f`

Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions.<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>user
## General Code Preferences

- Avoid ambiguity, for example:

```lua
-- 👎 if there's a bug in the regex, how would I know? I'd have to surmise from surrounding code 🤮
if line:match("^%s*$") then
    -- ...
end
```

```lua
-- 🙌 clear intent
local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$")
if is_blank_line then
    -- ...
end
```<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>user
## Lua Code Preferences

- use snake_case for functions and variables
- use PascalCase for classes
- use EmmyLua/LuaLS annotations to improve code completion
- use `luac` to check for syntax errors
- index/count variables end in `_base0` or `_base1` to indicate whether the loop starts at 0 (C-style) or 1 (Lua-style)

Examples:
```lua
---@param names string[]
---@param checker fun(name: string): boolean
---@return table<string, integer>
function process_names(names, checker) end

---@class WindowController
---@field window hs.axuielement
---@field title? string
---@field size { w: number, h: number }
local WindowController = {}
```<|im_end|>

You're using **nvim-cmp** with the **cmp-cmdline** source for command line completions.

Specifically:

- **Toggle**: `use_cmp_cmdline_search = true` (in `completions.lua` line 8)
- **Plugin**: `hrsh7th/nvim-cmp` + `hrsh7th/cmp-cmdline`
- **Config file**: `plugins/completions/cmdline_cmp.lua`

**Search mode** (`/`, `?`):
- Source: `buffer`
- Uses Tab/S-Tab for completion selection

**Command mode** (`:`):
- Sources: `path` + `cmdline` (commands, files)
- Same Tab/S-Tab mapping
- `<C-D>` / `<C-U>` to scroll half-screen through results
- Explicitly avoids Up/Down arrows because they conflict with command history navigation

There's also a commented-out section (`cmdline_nvim.lua`) that was an earlier attempt using Neovim's built-in cmdline completion (via `<C-n>` polling), but it was buggy and left as a reference.<|im_end|>

**Current state:** No, your cmdline completions don't include general workspace word completion.

**What you have:**
- `:` mode → `path` (file paths) + `cmdline` (commands, ex commands)
- `/` & `?` modes → `buffer` (current buffer only, for search highlighting)

**What's available but unused:** You have `hrsh7th/cmp-buffer` as a dependency, but it's not wired up for general word completion in cmdline mode.

**Notes:** I didn't find any explicit TODOs or comments explaining why you haven't enabled this. It looks like an oversight rather than a deliberate decision.

---

### To add workspace word completion to `:` mode:

You'd need to add `cmp-buffer` as a source, but **note**: `cmp-buffer` by default only searches the *current* buffer. To search across all open buffers (or the whole workspace), you have options:

**Option A:** Search all currently open buffers (fastest)
```lua
cmp.setup.cmdline(':', {
    mapping = mapping,
    sources = cmp.config.sources({
        { name = 'path' },
        { name = 'buffer' }  -- adds word completion from open buffers
    }, {
        { name = 'cmdline' }
    })
})
```

**Option B:** Search the entire workspace (slower, like LSP but for any word)
- Requires a plugin like `cmp-ripgrep` or `cmp-file` with custom word extraction
- Or use `nvim-cmp`'s built-in `buffer` source with `option.buffer_index = -1` (if supported in your version)

**Option C:** Use `cmp-vsnip` or similar if you want snippet-like abbreviation expansion (you noted this in a TODO: "try snippets with CLI! sounds like fish abbrs!")

Which approach do you prefer? I can wire it up.<|im_end|>

function.description: Run a process on this darwin machine
function.name: run_process

command_line.description: Shell mode: a shell command line executed via the system's default shell. Supports pipes, redirects, globbing. Cannot be combined with 'argv'.

stdin_text.description: Optional text written to STDIN (written fully, then closed). Useful for heredoc-style input or file contents.

argv.description: Executable mode: directly spawn a process. argv[0] is the executable, followed by arguments passed verbatim (no shell interpretation). Cannot be combined with 'command_line'.

timeout_ms.description: Optional timeout in milliseconds, defaults to 30,000ms

function.description: Retrieval tool (the R in RAG) for code and documents in the current workspace. Uses a vector store with embeddings of the entire codebase. And a re-ranker for sorting results.
function.name: semantic_grep

query.description: query text, what you are looking for

instruct.description: instructions for the query, explain the type of query

embed_top_k.description: number of embeddings matches to consider for reranking

filetype.description: limit matches to a vim compatible filetype. default includes all filetypes

top_k.description: number of matches, defaults to 5 which is usually plenty!

messages.content:
You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo.

## Ground rules when writing code:

- Follow the user's instructions carefully.
- Do not explain answers, just give me code.
- Respect indentation when modifying code.
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Tool use

Never modify files outside of the current working directory unless requested.

Current directory: ~/repos/github/g0t4/dotfiles

## Use `semantic_grep` to find code

The semantic_grep tool:

- has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase
- enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval)
- includes re-ranker
- it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it!
- while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg`

## Committing

- `git diff` is a sanity check!
- COMMIT when you're done.
- Use `--author="qwen3.6-35b-a3b<wes.mcclure+qwen3.6-35b-a3b@gmail.com>"`


## 🙌  TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS

- 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents
- 🙌 `fd` to search file names

## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS

If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom.

- ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f`

Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions.

## General Code Preferences

- Avoid ambiguity, for example:

```lua
-- 👎 if there's a bug in the regex, how would I know? I'd have to surmise from surrounding code 🤮
if line:match("^%s*$") then
    -- ...
end
```

```lua
-- 🙌 clear intent
local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$")
if is_blank_line then
    -- ...
end
```

## Lua Code Preferences

- use snake_case for functions and variables
- use PascalCase for classes
- use EmmyLua/LuaLS annotations to improve code completion
- use `luac` to check for syntax errors
- index/count variables end in `_base0` or `_base1` to indicate whether the loop starts at 0 (C-style) or 1 (Lua-style)

Examples:
```lua
---@param names string[]
---@param checker fun(name: string): boolean
---@return table<string, integer>
function process_names(names, checker) end

---@class WindowController
---@field window hs.axuielement
---@field title? string
---@field size { w: number, h: number }
local WindowController = {}
```

{"content":[{"text":"0","type":"text","name":"EXIT_CODE"},{"text":"local M = {}


function M.setup()
    local cmp = require('cmp')

    -- note: I like that it doesn't show until first char typed (by default)

    local suggested_mapping = cmp.mapping.preset.cmdline()

    function get_half_screen_height_lines()
        return math.floor((vim.o.lines - vim.o.cmdheight) / 2)
    end

    -- now I build a new mapping based on selected mappings from preset that I want
    local mapping = {}
    mapping['<Tab>'] = suggested_mapping['<Tab>']
    mapping['<S-Tab>'] = suggested_mapping['<S-Tab>']
    --
    -- CRAP that's right... these steal my ability to move up/down in command history when completion is visible.. which is like always
    -- mapping['<Up>'] = cmp.mapping(cmp.mapping.select_prev_item(), { 'c' })
    -- mapping['<Down>'] = cmp.mapping(cmp.mapping.select_next_item(), { 'c' })
    --
    -- move up/down by half screen height # of lines
    mapping['<C-D>'] = cmp.mapping(cmp.mapping.select_next_item({ count = get_half_screen_height_lines() }), { 'c' })
    mapping['<C-U>'] = cmp.mapping(cmp.mapping.select_prev_item({ count = get_half_screen_height_lines() }), { 'c' })
    -- vim.print(mapping)

    cmp.setup.cmdline({ '/', '?' }, {
        -- FYI appears that this can be different from mapping for cmdline below
        mapping = mapping,
        sources = {
            { name = 'buffer' }
        }
    })

    -- TODO try snippets with CLI! sounds like fish abbrs!
    --    https://github.com/hrsh7th/nvim-cmp/wiki/Example-mappings#ultisnips--cmp-cmdline
    cmp.setup.cmdline(':', {
        mapping = mapping,
        sources = cmp.config.sources({
            { name = 'path' }
        }, {
            { name = 'cmdline' }
        })
    })
end

You're using **nvim-cmp** with the **cmp-cmdline** source for command line completions.

Specifically:

- **Toggle**: `use_cmp_cmdline_search = true` (in `completions.lua` line 8)
- **Plugin**: `hrsh7th/nvim-cmp` + `hrsh7th/cmp-cmdline`
- **Config file**: `plugins/completions/cmdline_cmp.lua`

**Search mode** (`/`, `?`):
- Source: `buffer`
- Uses Tab/S-Tab for completion selection

**Command mode** (`:`):
- Sources: `path` + `cmdline` (commands, files)
- Same Tab/S-Tab mapping
- `<C-D>` / `<C-U>` to scroll half-screen through results
- Explicitly avoids Up/Down arrows because they conflict with command history navigation

There's also a commented-out section (`cmdline_nvim.lua`) that was an earlier attempt using Neovim's built-in cmdline completion (via `<C-n>` polling), but it was buggy and left as a reference.

messages.content:
**Current state:** No, your cmdline completions don't include general workspace word completion.

**What you have:**
- `:` mode → `path` (file paths) + `cmdline` (commands, ex commands)
- `/` & `?` modes → `buffer` (current buffer only, for search highlighting)

**What's available but unused:** You have `hrsh7th/cmp-buffer` as a dependency, but it's not wired up for general word completion in cmdline mode.

**Notes:** I didn't find any explicit TODOs or comments explaining why you haven't enabled this. It looks like an oversight rather than a deliberate decision.

---

### To add workspace word completion to `:` mode:

You'd need to add `cmp-buffer` as a source, but **note**: `cmp-buffer` by default only searches the *current* buffer. To search across all open buffers (or the whole workspace), you have options:

**Option A:** Search all currently open buffers (fastest)
```lua
cmp.setup.cmdline(':', {
    mapping = mapping,
    sources = cmp.config.sources({
        { name = 'path' },
        { name = 'buffer' }  -- adds word completion from open buffers
    }, {
        { name = 'cmdline' }
    })
})
```

**Option B:** Search the entire workspace (slower, like LSP but for any word)
- Requires a plugin like `cmp-ripgrep` or `cmp-file` with custom word extraction
- Or use `nvim-cmp`'s built-in `buffer` source with `option.buffer_index = -1` (if supported in your version)

**Option C:** Use `cmp-vsnip` or similar if you want snippet-like abbreviation expansion (you noted this in a TODO: "try snippets with CLI! sounds like fish abbrs!")

Which approach do you prefer? I can wire it up.
